legislation.title: Good Cause Eviction Law Sunset
legislation.summary: Current Good Cause Eviction law includes 10-year sunset clause, automatically expiring June 15, 2034 unless renewed by legislature
legislation.forwardLooking: Significant regulatory relief opportunity as law approaches expiration within typical investment hold periods
legislation.whyItMatters: Creates binary regulatory outcome - either law renewal with potential modifications or complete elimination of Good Cause rent growth constraints

rentControl.investmentImplications: State rent cap will apply starting 2027, limiting annual increases to 5% + CPI (max 10%). Current Los Angeles area CPI component is approximately 3.9%, creating effective cap around 8.9% annually once applicable.

rentControl.summary: Beverly Hills RSO applies only to buildings constructed before February 1, 1995
rentControl.applicability: Property permanently exempt from Beverly Hills RSO due to 2012 construction
rentControl.investmentImplications: Complete exemption from local rent stabilization requirements including registration, annual rent increase limits, and just-cause eviction protections. No local compliance obligations.

finalApplicability.classification: Currently unregulated, transitioning to state rent cap in 2027

finalApplicability.ageInterpretation: 14 years old as of 2026, crossing 15-year threshold in 2027
riskFlags: Transition from unregulated to regulated mid-hold period
finalApplicability.investmentImplications: Property currently enjoys maximum rent growth flexibility with no regulatory constraints. Beginning 2027, rent increases will be capped at AB 1482 formula (currently ~8.9% based on LA area CPI). This regulatory transition occurs mid-hold period for typical 5-year investment horizon. No risk of more restrictive local rent control due to Costa-Hawkins preemption. Proposition 33 defeated in November 2024, confirming Costa-Hawkins remains in effect.

legislation.title: Proposition 33 - Justice for Renters Act
legislation.summary: Would have repealed Costa-Hawkins Act, allowing local rent control on all housing types including post-1995 construction
legislation.forwardLooking: Defeated 62% to 38%, confirming Costa-Hawkins protections remain in place
legislation.whyItMatters: Failure preserves permanent exemption from Beverly Hills RSO for post-1995 properties

rentControl.statusLabel: Complete Ban on Local Rent Control
rentControl.summary: Illinois Rent Control Preemption Act (50 ILCS 825) prohibits all municipalities from enacting, maintaining, or enforcing any ordinance controlling rent amounts for residential or commercial properties
rentControl.applicability: Applies to all rental properties statewide
rentControl.investmentImplications: Provides maximum rent growth flexibility for landlords. No local jurisdiction can impose rent caps or stabilization measures. Landlords can adjust rents to market rates upon lease expiration without regulatory constraints.

rentControl.summary: Florida Statute 125.0103 prohibits local governments from imposing rent control ordinances except during declared housing emergencies requiring voter approval for maximum 1 year. HB 1417 (effective July 1, 2023) further preempts all local landlord-tenant regulations.
rentControl.applicability: Applies to all residential rental properties statewide
rentControl.investmentImplications: Property operates in fully deregulated market with no rent increase caps, providing maximum pricing flexibility. Recent legislative trend strengthens preemption rather than weakening it. 2018 construction date ensures no grandfathered local regulations apply.

finalApplicability.investmentImplications: Property benefits from Florida's landlord-friendly regulatory environment with no rent increase caps or mandatory notice periods beyond standard lease terms. Recent legislative trend strengthens rather than weakens preemption, providing exceptional regulatory certainty for investment planning.

legislation.title: HB 1417 - Residential Tenancies Preemption
legislation.summary: Preempted all local landlord-tenant regulations to state level, eliminating approximately 46 tenant protection ordinances across 35 jurisdictions including Miami-Dade's 60-day notice requirement for rent increases above 5%
legislation.forwardLooking: No pending legislation to repeal or modify preemption. Recent failed bills (HB 31, SB 64) show limited legislative appetite for rent control.

finalApplicability.primaryGoverningRule: Massachusetts Chapter 40P (1994) prohibits rent control statewide
finalApplicability.reasoning: Property is subject to complete state preemption of local rent control. Built in 1973, significantly pre-dates the 1994 statewide prohibition. No rent increase caps or regulatory constraints apply.
finalApplicability.ageInterpretation: Building age is irrelevant under current regulatory framework - all properties exempt from rent control regardless of construction date
riskFlags: 2026 statewide ballot initiative with 56-63% polling support
Proposed CPI + 5% cap would apply to this 1973 building
No vacancy decontrol in proposed measure

However, the 2026 ballot initiative poses material regulatory risk that would cap annual rent increases at the lower of CPI or 5%, with no vacancy decontrol between tenants. Polling data shows majority support (56-63%) for the measure. Investment returns could face significant constraints if ballot measure passes, creating binary regulatory outcome for underwriting purposes.

legislation.title: Massachusetts Rent Control Initiative (2026) - Petition No. 25-21
legislation.status: Pending - Signatures certified, legislative review ongoing
legislation.summary: Would repeal Chapter 40P statewide rent control ban and implement mandatory rent stabilization across all 351 cities and towns. Annual increases capped at CPI or 5%, whichever is lower. Rent in place as of January 31, 2026 becomes baseline for future increases. No vacancy decontrol - caps apply between tenants.
legislation.forwardLooking: Legislature has until May 5, 2026 to act on petition. If no legislative action, additional signatures required by July for November ballot placement. Polling shows 56-63% support.
legislation.whyItMatters: Would create strictest statewide rent control regime in US. Unlike pre-1994 system where cities opted in, this would mandate rent caps statewide. Property built in 1973 would be subject to caps with no new construction exemption.

baseCase.basis: Ballot measure fails - property remains unregulated with market-rate pricing
baseCase.regulatoryAssumption: Massachusetts Chapter 40P remains in effect, no rent control implementation

downside.basis: Ballot measure passes - rent increases capped at CPI or 5%, whichever is lower. Assuming CPI averages 3.5% based on recent trends
downside.trigger: 2026 ballot initiative passes with 56-63% polling support

upside.basis: Ballot measure fails and market conditions improve with continued deregulated environment
upside.trigger: Ballot initiative defeated, property investment increases due to regulatory certainty

underwritingScenarios.keyCondition: Investment returns dependent on November 2026 ballot outcome - verify acquisition timing relative to January 31, 2026 base rent date and monitor polling trends through election cycle.
